DESCRIPTION: The Grand Wash Bench Trailhead provides access to the twelve mile long trail that spans the Grand Wash Cliffs Wilderness Area. The landscape is characterized by rugged canyons, towering cliffs, and sandstone buttes.
Discover thousands of years of human history at the Little Black Mountain Petroglyph Site, located in the Arizona Strip.
The Nixon Sawmill was built in 1872 to provide lumber for construction of the Mormon Temple in St. George. An agency interpretive sign is installed at the historic site.
This remote homestead and ranch in Horse Valley on the Shivwits Plateau was established by Jonathan Deyo Waring in 1925. The site has cabins, corrals, fence lines, water tanks, and two holding ponds.

Tassi Ranch consists of a 1930s-era stone ranch house, a barn, corral, and a grove of huge cottonwoods beside a seasonal stream called Pigeon Wash. The remains of a holding pond and irrigation canal can be seen a short walk from the house.

The Grand Wash Bench Trailhead provides access to the twelve mile long trail that spans the Grand Wash Cliffs Wilderness Area. The landscape is characterized by rugged canyons, towering cliffs, and sandstone buttes.
The Paiute Wilderness is divided into two sections with the northern portion located on the Arizona Strip Field Office. The regions highest peak, Mt. Bangs, at 8,012 feet is nearby.
Pine Ranch is an early 1900s wood cabin that was originally built by the Mathis family and served as a ranch headquarters for many years before the lands were acquired by the federal government.
The Grand Gulch Mine is an abandoned copper mine from the late 1800s. The original stone buildings, bunkhouse, old steam engine parts, and two dump trucks remain on the site.

Witch pool is a public use site located on the northeast side of Mt. Trumbull. It may be the location of John Wesly Powell's visit with Southern Paiutes in 1870. Several petroglyphs are found at the site.
In 1879, the Canaan Company established a dairy ranch at Oak Grove. The remains of Oak Grove are an abandoned house in clearing amid a forest of Gambel oak.

The Paiute Wilderness is divided into two sections with the southern portion located within the Grand Canyon-Parashant National Monument. The deep canyons have several beautiful secluded places with water that attract campers and backpackers.

DESCRIPTION: The Nixon Sawmill was built in 1872 to provide lumber for construction of the Mormon Temple in St. George. The Uinkaret Pueblo site is near the sawmill site. This site was once a lively, domestic setting with food storage and a cooking area.

DESCRIPTION: Navajo Bridge Interpretive Center is the site of both the original and the new Navajo Bridges.  The center includes a rest area, bookstore, and Native American vendors on the east side of the bridge.  Open April through October.

Vermilion Cliffs National Monument Kiosk - This remote, unspoiled 280,000-acre national monument offers a variety of diverse landscapes - from the towering heights of the Paria Plateau and Vermilion Cliffs to the deep, winding chasms within Paria Canyon.
The Maze Rock Art Site got its name from the petroglyph featured prominently on the main panel.  Hike 0.5 miles east to view the petroglyphs.
PERMIT REQUIRED  Cottonwood Cove is an access point to Coyote Buttes South.  The large hoodoos north and west are called the Cottonwood Teepees.
PERMIT REQUIRED  Paw Hole is an access point to Coyote Buttes South.  There is no established trail.  Head north-northeast to explore around the hoodoos and buttes.
PERMIT REQUIRED  Lone Tree Trailhead is an undeveloped access point that provides entry for those that do not have a four-wheel drive vehicle to the southern part of Coyote Buttes.
West Bench Pueblo is the site of ancient dwellings used by the Ancestral Puebloans around A.D. 1050 - 1150.
At White Pocket in northern Arizona, windswept layers of orange, yellow, and white formations were created over time by mineral deposits and shaped by the elements.
Lees Ferry was the only crossing of the Colorado River until the construction of the Navajo Bridge. NPS campground, dumping station, and a public launch ramp.

Camp on river right with swift entry; only camp without train noise. Access to historic tunnel construction material out of the back of camp.
Small camp on river left with a small landing; access to trail.

Large camp on river right with abundant shade. Camp has a small bank to step up at most flows; look for eagles perched on opposite canyon walls.
Brushy flat bench on river left, with abundant train ambiance.

Large camp on river left with brushy landing and camping on both sides of the trail. This is the last camp in the Domiguez zone.
Large camp on river left with cottonwood trees, and opposite of sandstone wall. Good access to sand wash.
Small willowy landing on river left opens to large cottonwood-shaded camp. Popular with commercial groups.
